title: Between and within laboratory reliability of mouse behaviour recorded in home-cage and open-field

screening.reason: Reports behavioral outcomes in a home-cage setting, but lacks automated monitoring details.
matched_inclusion_criteria: Studies mice or rats in a home-cage or semi-natural automated monitoring setting
Reports behavioral, social, preference, dominance, activity, or interaction outcomes

overall_concept_summary: The study evaluates the reliability of mouse behavior recorded in both home-cages and open-field settings across different laboratories.
background_rationale: The paper aims to assess the consistency of behavioral measurements between different experimental conditions (home-cage vs. open-field) and laboratories, which is crucial for validating results across studies.
methods_summary: Mice were observed in both home-cage and open-field settings; however, specific automated monitoring tools are not detailed.
results_summary: Behavioral measures showed good reliability between home-cages and open-field tests within the same laboratory but less so between different laboratories.
conclusion_summary: The study highlights the importance of standardizing behavioral assessment methods to ensure consistency across studies.
relevance_to_review: Relevant, as it discusses behavioral outcomes in a home-cage setting, which is part of automated monitoring approaches for social behavior.
key_takeaways: Behavioral measures are more consistent within laboratories than between them.
objective: To evaluate the reliability of mouse behavior recorded in both home-cage and open-field settings across different laboratories.
population_or_sample: Mice observed in home-cages and open-field settings
intervention_or_exposure: Behavioral observation in both home-cage and open-field environments
methods: Observation of mouse behavior in home-cages and open-fields
outcomes_measured: Reliability of behavioral measures across different settings and laboratories
main_results: Behavioral measures showed good reliability within the same laboratory but less so between different laboratories.
conclusions: Standardization of behavioral assessment methods is necessary to ensure consistency in results across studies.
limitations: Lack of detailed automated monitoring tools and specific protocols used for observation
missing_information: Specific details on the automated monitoring systems used
